section.title: Elisha's Miracles
section.sectionRationale: A cluster of five episodes shows Elisha's ministry moving among ordinary, needy people: a widow drowning in debt, a childless woman of Shunem who receives and then loses and regains a son, a poisoned pot of stew, and a small provision of bread that feeds a hundred men with some left over. Each miracle answers real, desperate lack with the LORD's abundant provision through His prophet.
section.thesis: Through Elisha the LORD meets debt, barrenness, death, poison, and famine alike with overflowing provision, proving that no need is too small or too great for the God who sent him.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the widow's multiplied oil, verses 1 to 7; the Shunammite's promised and then restored son, verses 8 to 37; the purified stew, verses 38 to 41; and the multiplied bread, verses 42 to 44.

units.label: The widow's oil
units.note: sell the oil and pay your debt

verses.text: Now the wife of one of the sons of the prophets cried out to Elisha, "Your servant, my husband, is dead, and you know that your servant feared the LORD. And now his creditor is coming to take my two children as his slaves!"
"How can I help you?" asked Elisha. "Tell me, what do you have in the house?" She answered, "Your servant has nothing in the house but a jar of {{shemen|oil}}."
"Go," said Elisha, "borrow empty jars from all your neighbors. Do not gather just a few.
Then go inside, shut the door behind you and your sons, and pour oil into all these jars, setting the full ones aside."
So she left him, and after she had shut the door behind her and her sons, they kept bringing jars to her, and she kept pouring.
When all the jars were full, she said to her son, "Bring me another." But he replied, "There are no more jars." Then the oil stopped flowing.
She went and told the man of God, and he said, "Go, sell the oil, and pay your debt. Then you and your sons can live on the remainder."
One day Elisha went to Shunem, and a prominent woman who lived there persuaded him to have a meal. So whenever he would pass by, he would stop there to eat.
Then the woman said to her husband, "Behold, now I know that the one who often comes our way is a holy man of God.
Please let us make a small room upstairs and put in it a bed, a table, a chair, and a lamp for him. Then when he comes to us, he can stay there."
One day Elisha came to visit and went to his upper room to lie down.
And he said to Gehazi his servant, "Call the Shunammite woman." And when he had called her, she stood before him,

and Elisha said to Gehazi, "Now tell her, 'Look, you have gone to all this trouble for us. What can we do for you? Can we speak on your behalf to the king or the commander of the army?'" "I have a home among my own people," she replied.
So he asked, "Then what should be done for her?" "Well, she has no son," Gehazi replied, "and her husband is old."
"Call her," said Elisha. So Gehazi called her, and she stood in the doorway.
And Elisha declared, "At this time next year, you will hold a {{ben|son}} in your arms." "No, my lord," she said. "Do not lie to your maidservant, O man of God."
But the woman did conceive, and at that time the next year she gave birth to a son, just as Elisha had told her.

units.label: The Shunammite's son dies and is raised

verses.text: And the child grew, and one day he went out to his father, who was with the harvesters.
"My head! My head!" he complained to his father. So his father told a servant, "Carry him to his mother."
After the servant had picked him up and carried him to his mother, the boy sat on her lap until noon, and then he died.
And she went up and laid him on the bed of the man of God. Then she shut the door and went out.
And the woman called her husband and said, "Please send me one of the servants and one of the donkeys, that I may go quickly to the man of God and return."
"Why would you go to him today?" he replied. "It is not a New Moon or a Sabbath." "Everything is all right," she said.
Then she saddled the donkey and told her servant, "Drive onward; do not slow the pace for me unless I tell you."
So she set out and went to the man of God at Mount Carmel. When the man of God saw her at a distance, he said to his servant Gehazi, "Look, there is the Shunammite woman.
Please run out now to meet her and ask, 'Are you all right? Is your husband all right? Is your child all right?'" And she answered, "Everything is all right."

When she reached the man of God at the mountain, she clung to his feet. Gehazi came over to push her away, but the man of God said, "Leave her alone, for her soul is in deep distress, and the LORD has hidden it from me and has not told me."
Then she said, "Did I ask you for a son, my lord? Didn't I say, 'Do not deceive me?'"

So Elisha said to Gehazi, "Tie up your garment, take my staff in your hand, and go! If you meet anyone, do not greet him, and if anyone greets you, do not answer him. Then lay my staff on the boy's face."
And the mother of the boy said, "As surely as the LORD {{chai|lives}} and as you yourself live, I will not leave you." So he got up and followed her.
Gehazi went on ahead of them and laid the staff on the boy's face, but there was no sound or response. So he went back to meet Elisha and told him, "The boy has not awakened."
When Elisha reached the house, there was the boy lying dead on his bed.
So he went in, closed the door behind the two of them, and prayed to the LORD.
Then Elisha got on the bed and lay on the boy, mouth to mouth, eye to eye, and hand to hand. As he stretched himself out over him, the boy's body became warm.
Elisha turned away and paced back and forth across the room. Then he got on the bed and stretched himself out over the boy again, and the boy sneezed seven times and opened his eyes.
Elisha summoned Gehazi and said, "Call the Shunammite woman." So he called her and she came. Then Elisha said, "Pick up your son."
She came in, fell at his feet, and bowed to the ground. Then she picked up her son and went out.

units.label: The purified stew and the multiplied bread
units.note: there is nothing harmful in the pot

verses.text: When Elisha returned to Gilgal, there was a famine in the land. As the sons of the prophets were sitting at his feet, he said to his attendant, "Put on the large pot and boil some stew for the sons of the prophets."

One of them went out to the field to gather herbs, and he found a wild vine from which he gathered as many wild gourds as his garment could hold. Then he came back and cut them up into the pot of stew, though no one knew what they were.
And they poured it out for the men to eat, but when they tasted the stew they cried out, "There is {{maveth|death}} in the pot, O man of God!" And they could not eat it.
Then Elisha said, "Get some flour." He threw it into the pot and said, "Pour it out for the people to eat." And there was nothing harmful in the pot.
Now a man from Baal-shalishah came to the man of God with a sack of twenty loaves of barley bread from the first ripe grain. "Give it to the people to eat," said Elisha.
But his servant asked, "How am I to set twenty loaves before a hundred men?" "Give it to the people to eat," said Elisha, "for this is what the LORD says: 'They will eat and have some left over.'"
So he set it before them, and they ate and had some left over, according to the word of the LORD.

shemen.gloss: Oil, a staple good used for cooking, light, and anointing, and here the widow's only remaining asset. The LORD multiplies this small, ordinary resource until it exceeds every container she can find, stopping only when the vessels run out, not the oil itself.

ben.gloss: Son. A ben was central to a family's future, inheritance, and security in ancient Israel; the childless Shunammite's disbelief, do not lie to your maidservant, shows how far beyond hope Elisha's promise of a ben seemed to her.

maveth.gloss: Death. The prophets' cry, there is death in the pot, uses the same word Scripture uses for mortal danger generally; Elisha's simple act of adding flour turns maveth into safe food, a small but vivid picture of the LORD overturning death itself.

chai.gloss: Living or alive, the root behind the Shunammite's oath, as surely as the LORD lives. Her insistence not to leave Elisha until he came to her dying son echoes Elisha's own earlier vow never to leave Elijah, 2:2, 4, 6, binding master and disciple in the same persistent faith.

basic.context: This chapter gathers four scenes from Elisha's daily ministry among ordinary people. A prophet's widow, facing the loss of her sons into slavery for debt, receives multiplied oil from Elisha's word. A wealthy but childless woman of Shunem who has shown Elisha hospitality is promised, and then given, a son, who later dies of what sounds like heatstroke, and whom Elisha raises back to life after her urgent, faith-filled pursuit of him. A poisoned stew is purified with a handful of flour, and twenty loaves unexpectedly feed a hundred men with food left over.
basic.hermeneutics: These are historical accounts of specific miraculous acts through God's prophet; they are not formulas guaranteeing that every debt, illness, or hunger will be solved by ritual actions like pouring oil or adding flour. What endures is the character of the God who acts through Elisha: attentive to the desperate, generous beyond human resources, and able to reverse even death.
basic.originalLanguages: The widow's only asset is shemen, oil, which the LORD multiplies until every borrowed vessel is full. The childless Shunammite is promised a ben, son, a gift so far beyond her expectation that she initially disbelieves it. When death, maveth, appears to fill the prophets' pot, Elisha's simple act makes it safe again. And the Shunammite's urgent oath, as surely as the LORD, chai, lives, echoes the same persistent faith Elisha himself once showed toward Elijah.
basic.typology: The oil that overflows every vessel brought to it, the son given against all natural hope and then raised from death, and the food that multiplies beyond its natural supply all anticipate the greater abundance and resurrection life that Christ alone fully provides.
basic.christ: Elisha stretching himself over the dead child until warmth and life return anticipates Christ, who took on our death fully so that resurrection life might flow from Him into those who are spiritually dead, Ephesians 2:1-5. The multiplied loaves that fed a hundred with some left over directly foreshadow Jesus feeding thousands from a few loaves, John 6:1-13, revealing the same LORD of abundant provision at work in a greater Prophet.
basic.god: God is attentive to every scale of human need, from a widow's household debt to a family's grief over a dead child to hungry prophets facing a poisoned meal and a famine. He is not a distant deity but One who multiplies the little that is offered to Him and who has power even over death, displayed here through His servant Elisha before it is displayed fully in Christ.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 17:8-24 gives Elijah's earlier, similar provision of flour and oil for a widow and his raising of her son, the pattern Elisha now continues and extends. 2 Kings 8:1-6 later resumes the Shunammite woman's story, showing the LORD's continued care for her family. John 6:1-13 explicitly parallels the multiplication of loaves, and Mark 5:35-43 parallels Elisha's raising of the dead child.
basic.oneStory: These small, intimate miracles rehearse on a household scale the whole story the Bible tells: the LORD meets impossible debt, hopeless barrenness, sudden death, hidden poison, and real hunger with abundant, gracious provision, all anticipating the greater and final provision and resurrection secured for God's people in Jesus Christ.

context.literary: The chapter strings together four originally distinct episodes united by a single theme, Elisha meeting concrete human need with divine abundance, moving from a domestic financial crisis, to an extended narrative of promise, loss, and restoration, then to two brief communal meal miracles that bookend the whole Elisha cycle's emphasis on provision.
context.historical: Debt slavery, where children could be seized to pay a father's debts, was a recognized practice under ancient Near Eastern and Israelite law, though Israel's own law provided for release, Exodus 21:2, Leviticus 25:39-41, making Elisha's rescue of the widow's sons an act of covenant compassion. Shunem was a town in the Jezreel Valley; a woman of means building a dedicated guest room for a traveling prophet reflects genuine hospitality customs of the period.
context.canonical: These episodes closely parallel and extend Elijah's earlier ministry to a widow and her son in 1 Kings 17, showing Elisha as Elijah's true heir, exercising the double portion of spirit he requested in chapter 2, and anticipating Jesus' own multiplication miracles and raisings from the dead in the Gospels.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display, through a sequence of everyday crises, that the LORD who works through His prophet Elisha meets debt, barrenness, death, poison, and hunger with abundant provision, confirming that Elisha carries forward Elijah's God given authority and points ahead to a still greater deliverance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical miracles; it does not prescribe a ritual method, borrowing jars, adding flour, stretching over a body, for believers to replicate today. What endures is confidence in God's character as provider and life giver, not the particular physical actions Elisha used.

debates.question: Why did Elisha initially not know the cause of the Shunammite's distress, verse 27?

views.label: God selectively withholds knowledge from His prophets
views.case: The text explicitly states the LORD had hidden it from Elisha, showing that even genuine prophets do not possess automatic, unlimited knowledge but only what God chooses to reveal at a given moment, a check against over exalting the prophetic office.

views.label: A narrative device highlighting the woman's initiative and faith
views.case: By leaving Elisha initially unaware, the narrative foregrounds the Shunammite's own urgent, persistent pursuit of help, echoing Elisha's earlier refusal to leave Elijah, and shows that even in a crisis unknown to the prophet, faith presses forward toward God's appointed means of help.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that Elisha's power was never autonomous or automatic but entirely dependent on what the LORD chose to reveal and do through him, keeping the glory with God rather than the prophet.

study.originalLanguages: The widow's only remaining shemen, oil, becomes inexhaustible until she runs out of vessels, not oil. The childless Shunammite is promised a ben, son, so unexpected she cannot believe it, and yet her urgent oath after his death, as surely as the LORD, chai, lives, mirrors Elisha's own earlier devotion to Elijah. When maveth, death, seems to fill the prophets' cooking pot, a simple offering of flour turns it back to food, a small enacted parable of resurrection running through the whole chapter.

typology.type: Oil multiplied without limit from a small, ordinary supply
typology.fulfillment: The Spirit given without measure through Christ, and the abundant grace He supplies to all who trust Him, John 3:34, Ephesians 1:7-8.

typology.type: A dead child restored to life through the prophet's intimate identification with him
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own death and resurrection, by which He fully identifies with fallen humanity so that spiritual and physical resurrection life might flow to His people, Romans 6:5, 1 Corinthians 15:20-22.

typology.type: A small quantity of bread multiplied to feed many with abundance left over
typology.fulfillment: Jesus feeding the five thousand and four thousand from a handful of loaves, revealing His identity as the true bread of life, John 6:1-13, 35.

study.christ: Elisha lying over the dead boy until life returns, and the small store of bread that multiplies to feed a hundred with abundance to spare, both directly anticipate Jesus, the greater Prophet, who raised the dead by His own word and fed multitudes from a few loaves, revealing Himself as the true source of resurrection life and abundant provision, John 6:35, 11:25. Where Elisha needed to stretch himself out repeatedly and pray earnestly, Christ speaks and acts with His own inherent authority, showing that Elisha's miracles were signs pointing toward the greater Redeemer to come.
study.god: God attends to every scale of need, from a poor widow's debt to a barren woman's grief to a poisoned meal to a hungry crowd, and He supplies abundantly rather than merely adequately, oil beyond every vessel, bread with leftovers. He is also the God who gives and can reverse death, demonstrating through Elisha the same resurrection power that will be revealed fully in Jesus Christ.

crossReferences.note: Elijah's earlier ministry to a widow, including multiplied flour and oil and the raising of her son, is the direct pattern Elisha now continues as his prophetic heir.
The Shunammite woman's story continues later in the book, showing God's ongoing care for her family and land.
Jesus multiplies five loaves and two fish to feed five thousand with baskets left over, directly paralleling and surpassing Elisha's multiplication of bread.
Jesus raises Jairus's daughter from death, echoing the pattern of Elisha raising the Shunammite's son.

study.oneStory: Across four intimate scenes, the LORD meets crushing debt, childless grief, sudden death, hidden poison, and real hunger with abundant, personal provision through His prophet Elisha, rehearsing on a household scale the whole redemptive story that reaches its fullness in Jesus Christ, who feeds the hungry, raises the dead, and supplies grace upon grace to all who trust in Him.

quiz.q: How did Elisha help the prophet's widow facing the loss of her sons to a creditor?

options.text: He had her borrow many empty jars and pour her one remaining jar of oil into them until it miraculously filled every vessel, providing money to pay her debt.
options.note: Verses 2 to 7 record this exact miracle and its purpose.

options.text: He personally paid off her debt from his own resources.
options.note: The text describes a miracle of multiplied oil, not a direct cash payment from Elisha.

options.text: He told her to sell her sons into service temporarily.
options.note: The whole point of the miracle was to prevent her sons from being taken as slaves, not to arrange it.

options.text: He told her the debt would simply be forgiven with no action required.
options.note: Elisha instructed specific action, gathering jars and pouring oil, which she had to obey in faith.

quiz.q: What did Elisha promise the childless Shunammite woman, and how did she initially respond?

options.text: He promised she would hold a son in her arms within a year, and she disbelieved him, saying, do not lie to your maidservant.
options.note: Verse 16 records both the promise and her skeptical response.

options.text: She immediately believed and thanked him joyfully.
options.note: The text records her initial disbelief, not immediate joyful acceptance.

options.text: He promised her wealth rather than a child.
options.note: The specific promise concerned a son, not material wealth.

options.text: She had already asked Elisha for this exact promise.
options.note: The promise came unprompted; she had not requested a son from Elisha.

quiz.q: How did Elisha respond when the Shunammite's son died, and what did he do to restore him?

options.text: He went to the boy, prayed, and stretched himself over the body repeatedly until the boy sneezed seven times and opened his eyes.
options.note: Verses 33 to 35 describe this specific process leading to the boy's revival.

options.text: He simply spoke a word from a distance and the boy revived instantly.
options.note: The text describes physical, repeated, intimate action, prayer and stretching over the boy, not a distant spoken word alone.

options.text: He refused to help until the woman paid him.
options.note: No payment is mentioned; Elisha responded to her urgent plea and went with her immediately.

options.text: The boy could not be revived and remained dead.
options.note: The text explicitly records the boy's revival, sneezing seven times and opening his eyes.

quiz.q: How did Elisha resolve the danger of the poisoned stew at Gilgal?

options.text: He had flour thrown into the pot, after which there was nothing harmful in it and the people could eat safely.
options.note: Verses 40 to 41 record this exact resolution.

options.text: He commanded the men to simply throw the whole pot away.
options.note: Elisha's actual solution restored the food rather than discarding it entirely.

options.text: He identified the wild gourds and removed them one by one.
options.note: The text does not describe manual removal of the gourds; flour was added to the whole pot instead.

options.text: The stew remained dangerous and no one could eat it.
options.note: The text explicitly states there was nothing harmful in the pot afterward.

quiz.q: What happened when twenty loaves of barley bread were set before a hundred men?

options.text: According to the LORD's word through Elisha, the men ate and had food left over, despite the servant's doubt that so little bread could feed so many.
options.note: Verses 42 to 44 record the servant's doubt and the miraculous outcome exactly as Elisha declared.

options.text: The bread ran out before all the men could eat.
options.note: The text specifically states there was food left over, not a shortfall.

options.text: Elisha refused to distribute the bread at all.
options.note: Elisha explicitly commanded the bread be given to the people to eat.

options.text: The man from Baal-shalishah took the bread back before it could be shared.
options.note: The bread was given to Elisha specifically to be distributed to the people, and it was.

questions: The widow had almost nothing left, just one jar of oil, yet it became enough when placed in God's hands. What small resource do you have that you need to offer to God in faith rather than dismiss as insufficient?

The Shunammite woman refused to say anything was wrong until she reached the man of God, insisting everything is all right even in her grief. Where do you tend to hide your true distress instead of bringing it directly and urgently to God?

Elisha personally and physically identified with the dead child, stretching himself over him, before life returned. How does knowing that Christ fully entered our death shape your hope in your own suffering or grief?

The prophets almost ate poison without realizing it until someone cried out, and only Elisha's intervention made it safe. Where might you need discernment to recognize a hidden danger in your life, and where do you need God's provision to make something right again?

A small gift of twenty loaves fed a hundred men with some left over. What has God given you that seems too small to matter, and how might He be asking you to offer it anyway, trusting Him to multiply it?

translationNotes.note: Debt slavery for a father's unpaid debts was a real ancient Near Eastern and Israelite practice, though the Law of Moses provided limits and eventual release, Exodus 21:2, making Elisha's rescue an act of covenant mercy.

The husband's reference to the New Moon or Sabbath implies these were customary days for visiting a prophet, suggesting the Shunammite's urgent, unscheduled trip signaled something was seriously wrong even before she explained.
The instruction to tie up the garment renders a Hebrew idiom for preparing to move quickly, literally to gird up the loins, matching the urgency of the mission Elisha gives Gehazi.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 4:1-44. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
